FULL CONTENT:
ENERGY HARVESTING COMPILED BY HOWIE BAUM THIS PRESENTATION IS ABOUT ENERGY HARVESTING BEFORE WE TALK ABOUT IT, WE NEED TO TALK ABOUT SMALL ELECTRICAL SENSORS.
BEFORE WE TALK ABOUT THOSE, WE NEED TO DISCUSS THE INTERNET OF THINGS (IoT) ENERGY HARVESTING Energy harvesting is the process by which energy is obtained from external sources (such as solar power, thermal energy, wind energy, salinity (changes in the saltiness in ocean water) and kinetic energy, to operate low-energy electronics.
It is captured, and stored for small, wireless autonomous devices, like those used in wearable electronics and wireless sensor networks.
This is the first time in Human History that it is economical to obtain small amounts of energy now because the newer small sensors need very little power to work, and it also eliminates the need for a battery.
wind energy THE INTERNET OF THINGS WHAT IS THE INTERNET OF THINGS (IoT)?
The Internet of Things (IoT) is the network of physical objects— devices, appliances, vehicles, buildings and other items embedded with electronics, software, sensors, and network connections—that let these objects sense, collect, exchange data, and control devices.
• More than 27 billion IoT devices are active in 2020.
• It is expected that there will be 75 billion IoT devices in the world by 2025.
• 127 new devices are connected every second to the internet.
WHAT KINDS OF PRODUCTS AND PROCESSES CAN BE CONNECTED TOGETHER WITH THE INTERNET OF THINGS (IoT) ?
 Fitness trackers  Vehicles  Smart televisions  Smart doorbells and door locking systems  Light bulbs and lamps  Home security systems  Thermostats  Refrigerators  Laptops  Smartphones  Smart watches  Smart devices for Elder care  Smart cities  Smart Buildings  Smart Farming  Smart Animal Farming  Military  Energy & Water Utilities  Healthcare  Industrial Businesses  Retail Businesses Education – Partnership – Solutions Information Security Office of Budget and Finance There are three aspects to the IoT – 1) The central computing resource (the Cloud) 2) The access points and wireless connections (gateways) 3) The distributed sensors that continuously sense their environment for changes. Cloud computing is a type of computing that relies on shared computing resources rather than having local servers or personal devices to handle applications.
Applications, programs, memory storage, and other services are accessed via the Web. The services are delivered and used over the Internet and are paid for by the cloud customer on an as-needed or pay-per-use business model.
The Cloud actually is large data centers in buildings around the world that contain thousands of high speed computer servers We have developed “smart” technologies that are beginning to realize the vision for a Smart Planet, Smart Cities, Smart Transportation, Smart Grid, Smart Medicine, and more. We are on the verge of a growth of electronics not seen since the introduction of the cell phone and laptop computer. We are on the threshold of the Internet of Things, with trillions of connected devices to address nearly every facet of our lives, within the next 15 years.
Driving Forces of IoT 1. Sensor Technology – Tiny, Cheap, Variety 2. Cheap Miniature Computers 3. Low Power Needs 4. Capable Mobile Devices 5. Power of the Cloud for high level computing THE FOLLOWING ARE 12 AREAS WHERE THE INTERNET OF THINGS CAN REALLY HELP TO ORGANIZE SYSTEMS: SMART CITIES 1 Smart Parking Monitoring of parking spaces availability in the city.
2 Structural health Monitoring of vibrations and material conditions in buildings, bridges and historical monuments.
3 Noise Urban Maps Sound monitoring in bar areas and centric zones in real time.
4 Smartphone Detection Detect iPhone and Android devices and in general any device which works with WiFi or Bluetooth interfaces.
5 Electromagnetic Field Levels Measurement of the energy radiated by cell stations and WiFi routers.
6 Traffic Congestion Monitoring of vehicles and pedestrian levels to optimize driving and walking routes.
7 Smart Lighting Intelligent and weather adaptive lighting in street lights.
8 Waste Management Detection of rubbish levels in containers to optimize the trash collection routes.
9 Smart Roads Intelligent Highways with warning messages and diversions according to climate conditions and unexpected events like accidents or traffic jams.
Sensors are used in the fields to sense moisture and nutrients, efficient use of water for growing plants and determining the best use of fertilizers are some simple uses of IoT.
THE SOLAR AND WIND POWERED FARM https://www.youtube.com/watch?
v=GPproTNdIyw THE INTERNET OF THINGS AND CONNECTION TO OUR MEDICAL SYSTEMS SENSORS TYPES OF SENSORS USED FOR THE INTERNET OF THINGS https://www.youtub e.com/watch?reload =9&v=a_rhr4jtZtY&f eature=emb_logo 2 minutes SENSOR TECHNOLOGY Accelerometer (4mm diameter) Pulse Sensor $25 Force Sensor (0.1N – 10N) With the growth and development of these types of very small electronic technologies, it will provide more opportunities to put them into devices that interact with the IoT. MEMS (Micro Electro Mechanical Systems) NEMS (Nano electro Mechanical Systems) SOC (System on Chips) MEMS parts and chips WIRELESS SENSOR NETWORKS (WSNS) WSNs are networks of tiny, autonomous sensors with wireless transmission and sensing capabilities for a huge variety of applications, such as:  Healthcare  Entertainment  Work  Transportation systems  Industrial manufacturing automation  Smart electric grids.
Temperature and Humidity sensor (Light Dependent Resistor) (A circuit to measure temperature, humidity, pressure, and orientation) Passive Infra-Red Sensor ENERGY HARVESTING - (Changes in saltiness of Ocean water) TYPES OF ENERGY These are all of the main types of Energy around us.
The ones with a red circle by their name, are the main ones used for Energy Harvesting.
Movement of the Human Body (Radio Frequency) GSM stands for Global System for Mobile Communications nW is a nanowatt which is a billionth of a watt – extremely low power uW is a microwatt or a millionth of a watt mW is milliwatts or a thousandth of a watt This diagram shows the level of power that can be generated by different types of electronic devices.
nW is a nanowatt which is a billionth of a watt – extremely low power uW is a microwatt or a millionth of a watt mW is milliwatts or a thousandth of a watt The words at an angle of the diagram show the type of device that can produce the energy.
RF is radio frequency which is a very low level of energy that can be obtained by radio waves moving arounds us all of the time. OCEAN WAVE ENERGY HARVESTING DEVICES OCEAN TIDAL ENERGY HARVESTING VERTICAL AXIS WIND ENERGY HARVESTING TREE-SHAPED WIND TURBINES A French company called New Wind is installing tree-shaped wind turbines at the Place de la Concorde in Paris, France. They hope the trees can be used to exploit small air currents flowing along buildings and streets, and could eventually be installed in people’s yards and urban centers.
ENERGY HARVESTING FROM A PERSON’S MOVEMENTS The most fundamental and ideal solution is to scavenge energy from the environment and transform it into electricity Solar and wind can provide renewable and clean energy, but they cannot deliver continuous power because of their dependence on climatic conditions.
By contrast, human motion is present everywhere, exhibiting great potential to generate endless, clean, and sustainable biomechanical energy for smart electronics.
PAVEGEN The Pavegen company installed a 107-square-foot array in Bird Street in London, to harness and convert the power of footsteps into electricity, which will supply energy for lights and bird sounds in the area.
Bluetooth Low-Energy transmitters are also part of this array, so that users can interact with the array via apps. People can see their steps on the energy-harvesting pavement translate into discounts, vouchers, and clean electricity.
INTELLIGENT SOCKS The intelligent socks would assist wearable electronics to move toward digital human for diversified applications: • Energy harvesting for IoT applications • Human activity monitoring for VR fitness game • Gait identification for smart home application.
NEW TECHNOLOGIES FOR ENERGY HARVESTING USES OF POLYVINYLIDENE FLUORIDE PLASTIC Flexible piezoelectric generators (PEGs) are able to harvest mechanical energy and convert it to electricity.
Poly(vinylidene fluoride) and related fluoropolymers are the most common flexible piezoelectric materials used.
They are used in smart clothing, wearable electronics, on-skin and implantable sensors, as well as harvesting energy from the movement of vehicles, water and wind. POLYVINYLIDENE FLUORIDE (PVDF) PLASTIC STRAPS 1) Shoulder straps made of PVDF are strong, feels like Nylon, and they are piezoelectric, which means that an applied stress when walking, generates an electrical charge so it can charge portable electronic devices, like iPods and mobile phones. 2) PVDF also becomes super-sensitive to pressure when impregnated with a very small quantity of carbon nanotubes and then it produces a new generation of piezo-polymer, which are durable and can generate large quantities of electricity economically.
ELECTRICITY FROM DROPS OF WATER Japanese researchers focused on energy from the movement of liquid and created a device capable of generating electricity from the movement of a liquid droplet. The device was fabricated using flexible thin films made from molybdenum disulfide (MoS2) instead of graphene as the generator’s active material. This makes it possible to generate over five volts from a single liquid droplet. In contrast, graphene’s output voltage is limited to 0.1 volts, which is not enough to power electronic devices.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Q6hg KOMFU6M 2 minutes When water droplets slide down the device’s upper surface, electricity is generated from the natural energy that is produced and harvested GRAPHENE A SINGLE LAYER OF CARBON ATOMS USE OF GRAPHENE FOR ENERGY HARVESTING Graphene and graphene-based systems have recently been recognized as promising platforms for energy harvesting, microelectronic components and energy storage owing to their excellent electrical and thermal conductivity, outstanding mechanical properties, good chemical stability, and more.
Integration of energy harvesting systems relying on the graphene/graphene-based materials in contact with fluids is becoming significant. Researchers have designed a bracelet that harvests biomechanical energy from the wearer's wrist movements, which can then be converted into electricity and used to extend the battery lifetime of personal electronics or even fully power some of these devices.
CIRCUIT TRANSFERS GRAPHENE THERMAL MOTION INTO CURRENT This is a circuit capable of capturing graphene's thermal motion and converting it into an electrical current.
They found that freestanding graphene - a single layer of carbon atoms - ripples and buckles in a way that holds promise for energy harvesting.
"An energy-harvesting circuit based on graphene could be incorporated into a chip to provide clean, limitless, low-voltage power for small devices or sensors.
Impulse Excited Piezoelectric Generator Another possible transduction type is the piezoelectric effect. We developed piezoelectric energy harvesters with rotating proof masses, suitable for body worn devices. These have greatly increased power compared to electrostatic devices.
PIEZOELECTRIC SELF-POWERED WIRELESS SENSOR Based on the piezoelectric transduction and the rotating proof mass, this device was developed and will produce radio frequency (RF) energy when the mass is moved.
No batteries are needed Energy is transferred between the 2 copper rings show at the right.
AIRFLOW HARVESTING MICRO TURBINE We have developed a cm-scale shrouded wind turbine with a permanent magnet electromagnetic generator integrated into the shroud. With a cross-sectional area of only 8 cm2 this device is the smallest air-flow harvester to date that can generate mW power levels from low-speed air flows, and as such it provides a benchmark for flow harvesters at this scale. The device is aimed at wireless monitoring of air flow, temperature and/or quality in air conditioning ducts.
Stretchable biofuel cells extract energy from sweat to power wearable devices. PIEZOELECTRICITY?
Squeeze certain crystals (such as quartz) and you can make electricity flow through them.
The reverse is usually true as well: if you pass electricity through the same crystals, they will vibrate back and forth and can even create a spark to light something. Electronic drum sets use piezoelectric crystals to convert the energy of hitting them, to sound energy 85 SRI Shoe Energy Harvesting Shoe (400 mW) Self-winding Wristwatch (5 μW) Lightning Packs Energy Harvesting Backpack (16 W) 85 Overview of the State of the Art of Energy Harvesting Technologies 86 86 What is currently being done in Canada Bionic Energy Harvester 1.2 kg total (0.6 kg per leg) Comfortable 5-7 Watts without effort >10 Watts with effort Generative Braking Power & Control Module intermittent & variable power input efficiently charge a Lithium ion battery to store the energy. Harvesting Human Movement • Several possibilities that do not excessively burden the wearer Heel Strike and Shoe Flexure 2–20w Backpack Suspension and Padding 0.5–5w Limb Swing 0.2–3w Chest or Torso Expansion From Breathing or Routine Movement 0.1–1w Hand or Leg Cranked Generator for Emergency Back-up (Short-term) 10–100w Recent research studies have described the use of energy harvesting to power a pacemaker to eliminate the battery they use, so a future operation isn’t needed to replace the unit and battery.
The goal for energy harvesting in this case is to eliminate the need for a battery by generating electricity derived from an external source and possibly from the movement of the heart and surrounding organs !!
The body is full of various sources of energy that are waiting to be tapped for energy harvesting.
The most common energy sources are light, heat, vibration, movement, and Radio Frequency (RF) enery from the beating and pulsating heart and lungs The harvesting device is made by layering a piezoelectric material called lead zirconate titanate onto flexible silicone, adding electronics and batteries, and surgically implanting it on the organ without unduly restricting its motion.
These antennae can capture and harness Radio Frequency (RF) energy transmitted by such sources as radio and television transmitters, cell phone networks and satellite communications systems. The technique could provide a new way to power networks of wireless sensors, microprocessors and communications chips.
Georgia Tech School of Electrical and Computer Engineering professor Manos Tentzeris holds a sensor (left) and an ultra-broadband spiral antenna for wearable energy-scavenging applications. Both were printed on paper using inkjet technology.
2/1/2021 91 Researchers are devising a number of ways to generate mechanical energy from movements of the human body not only in standalone devices but also by integrating technology into fabrics.
One example of the latter, shown, is fabric that can simultaneously harvest energy from both sunshine and motion, developed by a team at the Georgia Institute of Technology. 2/1/2021 92 A smart wireless wearable sensor patch, programmed to monitor the user’s hydration levels, which derives all its power from the wearer’s own body heat.
When you are walking or jogging, the device becomes significantly more efficient, with its electricity-generating capacity increasing by 3 times. The new technology could be used to run low-power processors and sensors, such as hydration monitors, accelerometers, pressure sensors, EKG monitors, and temperature sensors The team is also developing a low-energy Bluetooth system that could allow the wearable device to send data directly to the user’s phone.

--- SOURCE 5: Energy Harvesting 101: A Beginner's Guide ---
URL: https://www.numberanalytics.com/blog/energy-harvesting-guide

SUMMARY:
Energy harvesting involves the conversion of ambient energy sources, such as solar, thermal, vibrational, and electromagnetic energy, into electrical energy . The process typically involves three stages: energy capture, energy conversion, and energy storage.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Energy harvesting captures ambient energy, converting it into electrical power for low-energy devices, eliminating battery reliance and enhancing sustainability across various applications.
Nanostructured materials, such as graphene and PVDF, exhibit exceptional properties for energy conversion, enabling efficient energy harvesting in flexible and wearable technologies.
Flexible and wearable devices utilize energy harvesting technologies to power sensors and electronics, allowing for continuous operation while enhancing mobility and user convenience.
